content_en:
The Muqaddimah fi Ulum al-Hadith (Introduction to the Sciences of Hadith) by Imam Abu Amr Uthman ibn Abd al-Rahman ibn al-Salah al-Shahrazuri (577–643 AH / 1181–1245 CE) is the foundational reference work in the science of hadith terminology (mustalah al-hadith). Composed initially as lecture notes for students at the Dar al-Hadith al-Ashrafiyyah in Damascus, it brought systematic organization to a vast and previously scattered field of knowledge, establishing the categories and terminology that all subsequent hadith scholars would use.

Ibn al-Salah begins with the most fundamental question: what is a hadith? In the broadest sense, 'hadith' refers to any statement, action, or tacit approval attributed to the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ, or to a Companion or Successor (in some usages). 'Khabar' (news/report) is often used synonymously, though some scholars distinguish them. 'Athar' (trace/remnant) typically refers to narrations from Companions and Successors specifically. The primary focus of the science of hadith, however, is the narrations of the Prophet ﷺ himself — his words (aqwal), his actions (af'al), and his tacit approvals (taqrirat).

The central organizing principle that Ibn al-Salah introduces in the opening chapter is the classification of hadiths by their level of authenticity. This is not an arbitrary academic exercise but a matter of the greatest religious importance: Islam is a religion whose laws, beliefs, and practices are derived from prophetic guidance, and the reliability of that guidance depends entirely on the reliability of its transmission. If a narration attributed to the Prophet ﷺ was never actually said by him, then acting upon it as a religious obligation or virtue is a form of bid'ah (innovation) — adding to the religion what Allah did not authorize.

The primary categories of hadith authenticity that Ibn al-Salah systematizes — building on the terminology developed by earlier scholars — are three: Sahih (sound/authentic), Hasan (good), and Da'if (weak). Below these major categories are numerous subcategories for different types of weakness and for special types of authentic narrations. The classification of any given hadith requires examining two dimensions: the chain of transmission (isnad) and the content of the text (matn). An authentic hadith must have a sound isnad — each narrator must be trustworthy (thiqah) and possess good memory (dabt) — and must have a sound matn — the text must not contradict the Quran, stronger hadiths, or established Islamic principles.

Ibn al-Salah's contribution was not to invent this terminology but to collect, organize, and systematize what earlier scholars had developed over centuries of practice. The science of hadith criticism (naqd al-hadith) had been practiced since the first generation — even the Companions verified narrations before acting on them — but it was in the great period from al-Shafi'i through al-Bukhari, Muslim, Abu Dawud, al-Tirmidhi, al-Nasa'i, and Ibn Majah (the authors of the six canonical collections, known as al-Kutub al-Sittah) that the formal methodology was developed. Ibn al-Salah's Muqaddimah captured that methodology at its mature stage and transmitted it to every subsequent generation.
